images.caption: Figure 1:Top: A student spots his homework mistake by comparing his work to the reference answer.Bottom: An LLM’s response generation probabilities shift when guided by a reference answer.
Figure 2:Impact of contrastive disagreement on token correctness (Accuracy@16).
Figure 3:The illustration ofCPO. Prior and reference-guided posterior likelihoods are contrasted to shape token-level advantages.
Figure 4:Comparison of different posterior designs (πpost\pi_{\text{post}}) under a unified CPO framework.

Figure 5:Training dynamics of Qwen2.5-Math-7B under CPO and GRPO. (a-b) Greedy decoding accuracy on in-domain AIME2025 and out-domain MMLU-PRO across training steps; (c-d) Pass@K performance (K=1 to 16) on both test sets, with Qwen2.5-Math-7B results (Base) included for comparison; (e) Training entropy and (f) average response length over the course of training.
Figure 6:Training dynamics of CPO with differentα+:α−\alpha^{+}:\alpha^{-}ratios. (a) training entropy; (b)/(c) Pass@KKon AIME2025 and MMLU-PRO.

Figure 7:Word clouds of 100 tokens with top absolute disagreement (CPO) and entropy: (a)CPO - Entropy: tokens only detected byCPO; (b)CPO∩\capEntropy: tokens detected by both; and (c)Entropy - CPO: tokens detected only by entropy.
